front.title: RCR/1: Receipt Commitment Record Format for AI Agent Actions

abstract.t: This document specifies RCR/1 (Receipt Commitment Record version 1), a canonical JSON format for representing vendor-native receipts produced by AI agent actions. RCR/1 defines a structured record with mandatory fields for source identity, action classification, effect status, cryptographic evidence, chain linkage, and bounded claims. Records are linked via hash chaining using SHA-256 with RFC 8785 canonical JSON serialization. RCR/1 addresses record-keeping requirements from the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) Article 12, ISO/IEC 42001, SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria, and HIPAA audit controls. The format is vendor-neutral and designed to translate receipts from any external system into a single verifiable canonical form.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Autonomous AI agents increasingly act on behalf of humans and organizations across heterogeneous external systems: payment processors, identity providers, ticketing platforms, healthcare electronic data interchange, e-commerce checkouts, government portals, and many others. Each such system produces its own receipt or confirmation artifact in a vendor-specific format, with vendor-specific identifiers, signatures, and semantics. The result is that the proof that an agent performed a given action is fragmented across dozens of incompatible record formats, which makes downstream verification, regulatory attestation, and dispute resolution operationally infeasible.

This document specifies RCR/1, a canonical JSON receipt format designed to translate any vendor-native receipt into a single verifiable structure. An RCR/1 record carries a bounded set of required fields, an explicit claim strength taxonomy that prevents over-attestation, optional cryptographic evidence from the originating vendor, and a chain pointer linking it to a prior receipt. Records are hashed with SHA-256 over RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS) output to produce a stable receipt identifier.

RCR/1 is complementary to draft-sharif-agent-audit-trail ("Agent Audit Trail", AAT). AAT specifies an internal, agent- centric log of the reasoning, tool calls, and decisions an agent makes within its own execution environment. RCR/1 specifies an external translation of the receipts that an agent's actions cause to be issued by third-party systems. The two formats are designed to coexist: an AAT entry MAY reference an RCR/1 record by its receipt hash, and an RCR/1 record MAY reference an AAT entry as the originating decision.

section.name: Conventions and Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.
The following terms are used throughout this document.

dl.dd: A structured record attesting that a specific action occurred in an external system, formatted according to this specification.
Software that translates a vendor-native confirmation artifact into an RCR/1 record.
A typed assertion within a receipt, qualified by a strength level defined in .
The party that produced and signed the RCR/1 record. The issuer is typically the operator of the adapter, not the underlying vendor.
The agent or principal on whose behalf the underlying action was performed.
The observable outcome of the action: success, failure, partial, or pending.
An ordered append-only sequence of RCR/1 records linked by inclusion of each record's receipt hash in the next record's previous_receipt_hash field.

section.name: RCR/1 Record Format
section.t: An RCR/1 record is a JSON object. All field names are lowercase ASCII. All timestamps are RFC 3339 strings in UTC with second precision and a trailing "Z". All identifiers are UUIDs as specified in unless explicitly noted otherwise.

section.name: Required Fields
section.t: Every RCR/1 record MUST contain the following fields.

dl.dd: String. MUST be the literal "rcr/1".
String. A UUIDv4 unique to this record.
String. RFC 3339 UTC timestamp of issuance.
Object with required string fields "id" and "name", identifying the party producing this record.
Object with required string field "agent_id" and optional string field "principal_id".
Object identifying the originating vendor system, with required string fields "vendor" and "endpoint".
Object with required string fields "type" and "category". Defined categories are listed in .
String. One of "success", "failure", "partial", "pending".
Object. See .
Object. See .
Array. See .

section.name: chain Object
section.t: The chain object links a record to its predecessor in an append-only sequence.

dl.dd: String. UUIDv4 identifying the chain.
Integer. Zero-based ordinal of this record.
String. The 64-character lowercase hexadecimal SHA-256 of the prior record's canonical form, or the literal "GENESIS" if sequence is 0.

section.name: evidence Object
section.t: The evidence object carries cryptographic artifacts obtained from the underlying vendor or notarization service.

dl.dd: String. See .
Optional string. Base64url of any signature obtained from the vendor.
Optional string. IANA-registered signature algorithm identifier if vendor_signature is present.
Optional string. Base64url of an RFC 3161 TimeStampToken binding the receipt hash to a trusted time.
Optional object describing an external anchor (for example a transparency log inclusion proof).

section.name: claims Array
section.t: The claims array carries typed factual assertions about the action. Each element is an object with the following fields.

dl.dd: String. The semantic type of the claim, for example "amount_charged", "delivery_address_hash", "appointment_time".
String, number, boolean, or null.
String. One of the seven levels defined in .
Optional string. Free-form reference into the originating vendor artifact (for example a JSON pointer or vendor field name).

section.t: A receipt MUST NOT include any claim at a strength level higher than the adapter can substantiate from the underlying vendor evidence.

section.name: Claim Strength Taxonomy
section.t: RCR/1 defines a seven-level taxonomy. Implementations MUST use one of these literals and MUST NOT introduce private strength levels.
Full semantics are given in .

section.name: Witness Classes
section.t: The witness_class field in the evidence object classifies the strongest available cryptographic binding. Defined values are:

dl.dd: No vendor cryptographic evidence available.
Shared-secret HMAC computed by the vendor.
Asymmetric signature from a vendor key.
RFC 3161 timestamp from a trusted authority.
Inclusion in a public append-only log.
Inclusion in a public distributed ledger.

section.name: Receipt Hash Computation
section.t: The receipt hash is computed as follows:

ol.li: Construct the receipt JSON object containing all required fields described above.
Serialize the object using RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme .
Compute SHA-256 over the resulting octet sequence.
Encode the digest as 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters.

section.t: The receipt hash is not stored within the record it identifies; it is carried in the next record's previous_receipt_hash field and in any external index.

section.name: RFC 8785 JCS
section.t: RCR/1 canonicalization is the JSON Canonicalization Scheme with the additional constraints stated in this section. The combined profile is named "rcr-jcs-1".

section.name: No Float Rule
section.t: RCR/1 records MUST NOT contain JSON numbers with a fractional component or exponent. Monetary amounts MUST be encoded as integers in the smallest indivisible unit (for example cents) alongside an explicit currency claim. This eliminates floating-point canonicalization ambiguity entirely.

section.name: Binary Encoding
section.t: All binary values (signatures, timestamps, hashes encoded as bytes, raw certificates) MUST be encoded as base64url without padding, per Section 5. Hex-encoded digests intended for human readability MUST use 64 lowercase characters.

section.name: Receipt Hash Algorithm
section.t: The receipt hash algorithm in RCR/1 is SHA-256. A future RCR profile MAY define an additional algorithm; such a profile MUST use a distinct version literal in the version field.

section.name: Claim Strength Taxonomy
section.t: This section defines the seven claim strength levels in full. Levels are listed from weakest to strongest. A receipt MAY contain claims at multiple levels.

section.name: customer_asserted
section.t: The value was supplied by the principal or the agent and has not been confirmed by any external party. Example: an end user types a shipping address into a form; the address is recorded as customer_asserted until the carrier confirms it.

section.name: vendor_lookup_token
section.t: The vendor returned an opaque identifier that can be re-presented to the vendor to retrieve the underlying fact. The vendor has not cryptographically signed anything. Example: a Stripe charge ID returned without a webhook signature.

section.name: vendor_signed
section.t: The vendor provided a cryptographic signature, HMAC, or webhook signature over the asserted fact. Example: a Stripe webhook signed with the standard webhook secret, validated by the adapter.

section.name: notarized
section.t: The fact is bound to a trusted timestamp from an RFC 3161 TSA or equivalent, and that timestamp covers the receipt hash. Example: a FreeTSA TimeStampToken over the SHA-256 of the RCR/1 record.

section.name: qualified_seal
section.t: The fact is signed under a qualified electronic signature or qualified electronic seal as defined by the eIDAS Regulation, or an equivalent national scheme. Example: a qualified seal applied by a trust service provider.

section.name: regulator_attested
section.t: The fact has been attested by a regulator or accredited third party operating under a published audit framework. Example: a healthcare claim acknowledgement from a CMS-accredited clearinghouse.

section.name: public_ledger
section.t: The fact, or its hash, has been included in a publicly verifiable append-only log or distributed ledger such that any third party can independently confirm inclusion. Example: a Sigstore Rekor log inclusion proof or a transaction in a public blockchain.

section.name: Personal Data Hashing
section.t: Personal data, as defined by GDPR Article 4(1) or equivalent regimes, MUST NOT appear in plaintext in an RCR/1 record. Such values MUST be replaced with a SHA-256 hash of the canonical form, optionally salted with a per-subject salt held separately. The corresponding claim type SHOULD carry a "_hash" suffix, for example "shipping_address_hash".

section.name: PHI
section.t: Protected Health Information, as defined by 45 CFR 164.514, MUST NOT appear in plaintext in an RCR/1 record. Implementations operating in HIPAA-regulated environments MUST treat all health-related claim values as PHI and apply the hashing requirement in . Adapters MUST NOT include free- text clinical notes in any claim, even hashed.

section.name: Attorney-Client Privilege
section.t: Adapters that observe communications protected by attorney-client privilege or equivalent professional secrecy regimes MUST NOT encode the content of those communications into claim values. Only the existence, time, and counterparty (hashed per ) of such communications MAY be recorded.

section.name: Replay Attacks
section.t: An adversary in possession of a valid RCR/1 record could attempt to replay it into a different chain. The combination of chain_id, sequence, and previous_receipt_hash binds each record to a specific position in a specific chain; verifiers MUST reject a record whose previous_receipt_hash does not equal the hash of the actual prior record at sequence minus one in the same chain.

section.name: Key Rotation
section.t: Issuer keys SHOULD be rotated on a published schedule. A rotation event SHOULD itself be recorded as an RCR/1 record at the head of the next chain segment, and the new issuer public key SHOULD be discoverable via a stable well-known endpoint controlled by the issuer. Verifiers encountering a record signed under a superseded key MUST consult the issuer's published rotation history before treating the record as invalid.

section.name: HMAC Secret Exposure
section.t: A claim at strength vendor_signed based on a shared HMAC secret is only as strong as the secret. If the secret leaks, any party with the secret can forge records. Adapters SHOULD prefer asymmetric signatures over HMAC where the vendor supports them, and MUST treat HMAC secrets as bearer credentials with strict access controls and rotation.

section.name: Post-Quantum
section.t: RCR/1 mandates SHA-256 for receipt hashing and does not constrain vendor signature algorithms. SHA-256 is believed to retain at least 128 bits of preimage security against currently anticipated quantum attacks, which is acceptable for the chain-binding use case. Asymmetric signatures used by vendors or issuers may need to migrate to post-quantum algorithms over the lifetime of long-lived archives. A future version of this specification MAY mandate a post- quantum hash or signature; such a version will use a distinct version literal.

section.name: Media Type Registration
section.t: IANA is requested to register the following media type in the "Media Types" registry, per .

dl.dd: application
rcr+json
N/A
version (default "rcr/1")
binary (UTF-8)
See of this document.
Records MUST conform to the canonicalization profile rcr-jcs-1 defined in .
This document.
dl.dt: Applications that use this media type:
dl.dd: AI agent governance systems, audit trail processors, compliance reporting pipelines.
None.
dl.dt: Person and email address to contact for further information:
dl.dd: Wesley Snow, wes@rankigi.com
COMMON
None
Wesley Snow
IETF

section.name: Extension Namespace Registry
section.t: IANA is requested to establish the "RCR/1 Extension Namespace" registry. Entries register a reverse-DNS prefix that an organization may use to namespace private extension fields within an RCR/1 record. Registration policy is First Come First Served per . Each entry contains:

dl.dd: A reverse-DNS prefix, for example "com.example".
Name and email of the registrant.
A stable URL describing the extension.

section.t: Field names within a registered namespace MUST be prefixed with the registered prefix followed by a colon, for example "com.example:internal_ref". Verifiers that do not recognize a namespaced field MUST preserve it byte-for-byte when re-canonicalizing.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
A Universally Unique IDentifier (UUID) URN Namespace
The Base16, Base32, and Base64 Data Encodings
Internet X.509 Public Key Infrastructure Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP)
Media Type Specifications and Registration Procedures
Guidelines for Writing an IANA Considerations Section in RFCs
Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence (Artificial Intelligence Act)
ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Information technology, Artificial intelligence, Management system
Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy
HIPAA Security Rule, 45 CFR Part 164 Subpart C

author.organization: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

section.name: Appendix A. EU AI Act Article 12 Mapping
section.t: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Article 12 requires that high- risk AI systems be designed to automatically record events ("logs") over the lifetime of the system. RCR/1 addresses the external-effect portion of that requirement as follows.

dl.dd: An adapter MUST emit an RCR/1 record for every external action attributable to the AI system.
The issued_at field provides the wall-clock binding required to demonstrate operation over a stated period.
The source.vendor and source.endpoint fields identify the external database or system involved in each action.
The claims array carries hashes of input values where direct retention is prohibited by .
The subject.principal_id field, hashed per where required, identifies the natural person on whose behalf the action was taken.

section.name: Appendix B. ISO/IEC 42001 Mapping
section.t: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 control objectives addressed by RCR/1:

dl.dd: The chain object and SHA-256 receipt hashing provide tamper-evident operation logs.
The claim strength taxonomy in explicitly bounds the confidence that may be asserted for any data point.
The effect field provides structured success/failure telemetry suitable for management review.
The source object and evidence.witness_class field document the dependence on each third party and the strength of its attestation.

section.name: Appendix C. SOC 2 Mapping
section.t: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria addressed by RCR/1:

dl.dd: Continuous emission of RCR/1 records provides the monitoring evidence required by CC7.2.
The effect field combined with the chain pointer permits forensic reconstruction of any incident.
The no-float rule and rcr-jcs-1 canonicalization eliminate numeric drift in monetary and quantitative claims.
The issuer and subject objects record the principal and agent identities for each external action.
